What confronts us in Daniel's vision is not a comfortable promise of steady progress toward God's kingdom. Instead, we witness the violent, grinding succession of earthly powers—beasts that devour, crush, and trample. The kingdoms rise from the sea, turbulent and chaotic, each more terrible than the last. These are not abstract theological symbols; they represent real empires that will inflict real suffering on real people. The fourth beast, with its iron teeth and bronze claws, stands apart in its savagery, and from within it emerges a little horn that will wage open war against the saints of the Most High. This is what Daniel sees, and this is what troubles him deeply. Before we rush to comfort ourselves with the promise of God's kingdom, we must sit with the weight of what precedes it: centuries of oppression, kingdoms that will "devour the whole earth," and a final adversary who will persecute God's people and even attempt to alter the very laws and seasons that God has appointed. This is not a vision for the faint-hearted, but for those who will endure.

Yet in the midst of this terror comes a pivot so stunning it reshapes everything. The Ancient of Days takes His seat, clothed in white, His throne ablaze with fire. The books are opened. Justice is coming—not as an afterthought, but as the inevitable response to the arrogance of kingdoms that have set themselves against the Most High. The fourth beast is slain. The blazing fire consumes it. And then, in a moment of profound significance, the Son of Man appears, coming with the clouds of heaven, and is brought before the Ancient of Days. He receives dominion, glory, and kingship over all peoples and nations. His kingdom will not fade like the empires of men; it will never be destroyed. This is not merely hope. This is the declaration that history itself has already been decided by God.

The angel's interpretation makes the stakes unmistakably clear: the saints will inherit the kingdom. Not the powerful kingdoms of earth. Not those who compromise or accommodate themselves to the beasts. But the saints of the Most High—those who remain faithful even as the little horn prevails against them, even as they are given into his hand for a season. Notice this: the text does not promise that the saints will avoid oppression. It promises that oppression is temporary, measured, and ultimately powerless to prevent what God has already determined. The little horn speaks arrogantly against the Most High, but his dominion will be taken away and completely destroyed forever. The saints' dominion, by contrast, is everlasting. When the court convenes, judgment comes swiftly in their favor.

This vision calls us not to passivity but to a radical reorientation of loyalty. We live between the vision and its fulfillment, in a time when beasts still devour and the little horn still speaks. But we see what Daniel saw: that the Ancient of Days has already taken His seat, that Christ has already been exalted, and that the outcome is certain. Our faithfulness now, in a world dominated by earthly powers, is not an investment in hope—it is a response to a victory already won. The question Daniel's chapter presses into our hearts is this: will you remain loyal to a kingdom that cannot yet be seen, while the beasts that you can see press in with all their fury?
